question_text: A speaker produces a sound wave in air. When the same speaker is submerged underwater, the frequency remains the same but the wavelength increases. Explain why the wavelength changes, using the wave equation (v = f × λ).
correct_answer: Sound travels faster in water (1480 m/s) than in air (340 m/s). Since v = f × λ and frequency stays constant, wavelength must increase proportionally to the increase in velocity.
explanation: The wave equation tells us that wavelength = velocity ÷ frequency. When velocity increases but frequency stays the same, wavelength must get bigger. It's like a factory speeding up production at the same rate—items must spread further apart!
